speaker: interviewer
text: Today's date is August 27, 1996. Survivor is Mark Weidman. The interviewer is Ed Lessing. The interview is conducted in Spring Valley, New York, in Rockland County in the USA. The language will be in English. My name is Ed Lessing. That's spelled L-E-S-S-I-N-G. Today's date is August 27, 1996. I'm conducting an interview with Mr. Mark Weidman. The interview is being conducted in Spring Valley, New York, in the USA. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The language will be English. Would you please give me your name and spell it, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Mark Weidman. M-A-R-K W-E-I-D-M-A-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have any other names?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have a nickname that was called back home. And that nickname was Musio, M-U-S-I-O.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you please give me your birth date?

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old are you at this point?

speaker: interviewee
text: 73 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you give me the city and the country of your birth, and please spell the city name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Rolow, Poland. R-O-L-O-W, Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Weidman, shall we begin by going back to the time before the war? Would you please relate a little bit about your family life [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] with your-- with your parents and any siblings. Where did you live and what kind of a house, et cetera, et cetera.

speaker: interviewee
text: I had-- my childhood was very happy. They were very happy years. I lived in Schodnica. It was a small town, rather-- rather a larger village, district of Drohobycz. There was a petroleum industry there, and my family was involved in that-- in the-- in the oil fields. My father was a manager of an oil field. I had-- we had a private house [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] where my grandparents lived with us. Also I had a sister, four years my junior. And my-- my younger years were full of dreams, full of hope. And I went to school, to public school, in the morning. In the afternoon, I had to go to an orthodox cheder school to learn Hebrew. I-- I finished six grades of elementary school of that public school. Those years were real-- really my happiest years of my life. I looked up to the future with-- with hope and was sure that everything would go my way because financially we were very well off. And so till the age of about [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] 14, I spent in Schodnica. Had a lot of friends. We were going on vacations, usually to my grandparents' house which was a different village and different area in Poland, [POLISH], where my-- my mother was born, and my grandparents, my maternal grandparents, lived there. Because in Schodnica, we had our paternal grandparents with us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you describe a typical day, and a regular work-- a business day, a work day, in your family, for instance. What-- where did people go, and where did you go, where did your parents, what did they do.

speaker: interviewee
text: My father, as my grandfather, they were very early risers. And I remember my grandfather, he used to rise-- he was a very pious person. He used to rise in the wee hours. And as a matter of fact, was waking me up saying his prayers. And he was going to the field-- we had also our own fields, agricultural fields, where we grew our potatoes and hay and wheat and so forth.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he was going-- after prayers, he was going there. Sometime I was keeping him company. And I was coming back, I remember they were very pleasant memories. And a big wagon, horse drawn wagon, with hay. And on top of the hay-- of that wagon, of the hay, I was with the--with the driver, with my grandfather, and myself coming home. I still remember very vividly.

speaker: interviewee
text: Also-- I also had a lot of friends, and we were playing together before I went to school. Simple, simple games, but very enjoyable games.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you describe a little bit about your father's profession and-- and-- and his life.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my father was a manager-- he was a technical expert in the oil fields. And he managed oil fields. As a matter of fact, when there were problems in other oil fields, they always consulted-- my father was like a consultant, to solve problems. Like say some-- some instruments fell down the shaft. So he-- he was the only one in the area who knew how to-- how to pull them out, how to drag them out. So my father was a very capable man. He was very hard working. He was going to the oil fields early in the morning after prayers, and he was coming home sometimes late at night.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I really saw my father more on the Sabbath than during the week. Because when he came home, I was already probably sleeping. And-- but Friday night, the Sabbath, we spent together. They're very joyful memories. Did your mother go out to work also, or was she a house wife?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. My mother-- my mother did not work. My mother worked with the house-- housekeeper. She-- house wife. She took care of the family, took care of the house. We also had help. We had a maid, a servant. We had our-- our own cows. So the servant was also milking the cows, because we had our milk. We made our own cheese, our own butter.

speaker: interviewer
text: That makes me think, was the-- was there a barn next to your house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we had a barn. We had a barn. We had two cows there. A couple of goats. And we-- we raised them ourselves. And we had our own milk, our own dairy products from our cows.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you live in-- in town? Could you describe the house and-- and the location, and what it looked like a little bit?

speaker: interviewee
text: The house was a wooden house. Like all the wooden-- wooden houses. We had a-- an entrance foyer. Then there was a kitchen. Pretty large kitchen with a stove, a wood-- wood stove, wood burning stove. Because we had no-- we had also outside of the house, we had a-- a well, a water well. We had to drag in the water into the house and keep in a-- in a vessel. And from there, we drank the water or cooked for-- for cooking. And it was a-- usually the maid was-- bring in the water to the house. Later-- later on in war times, I-- I was the one to drag in the water, bring the water to the house.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about the animals that-- you said you had a cow and goats.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, they had--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were they housed?

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- they had-- the-- we had a stable outside the house. We had our farm buildings. We had a stable. We kept the two cows. As a matter, if we raised our own cows. When the calf was born, when the calf was a nice calf, we raised it ourselves to become a cow. And I still remember, we had one cow that we raised ourselves. We kept it till the start of the war. And we could never-- we could never, never slaughter them and eat them because they were like part of our family. I remember when they came from the pasture, she was coming to the kitchen window. She recognized all of us. She was staying and looking at us through the window. And when-- when she got through it, then she went to the stable to eat her hay, to be milked. It was very-- and I remember from when the war started when we couldn't-- we had to move from there. So we gave away the cow instead of slaughtering her. We gave her away to a Ukrainian farmer, he should take care of her.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell-- tell me a little bit about your life in school. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] What sort of school was it? What sort of playmates did you have? What did you learn?

speaker: interviewee
text: The public school doesn't have very fond memories. It was a public school-- so-called public school-- but we were in a minority. Jews were in a minority. And there were Polish students, Ukrainian students. Some of them pretty anti-Semitic, and they made always anti-Semitic remarks towards us. And we were very touched by it. That was before the war. Remember the-- the school-- the school day, we started with a prayer. There was a-- there was a cross hanging on the wall, and-- but we were silent. We did not participate in the prayers. And we were in-- we-- we were getting derogatory remarks by the-- by the non-Jewish students regarding not-- the non-participation in the prayers.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many Jewish children were there in your class?

speaker: interviewee
text: The class was-- the class had approximately 30 students in public school. I would say we were evenly divided, by 10 Jewish students, 10 Polish, and 10 Ukrainian. Approximately. Evenly divided population. And that reflected also in the classroom. And the teachers were-- some of them were Jewish teachers, women. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And some were Polish. There was one Ukrainian teacher, I remember, too. Because Ukrainian was taught also as a-- as a language a couple hours a week. We had to write and read Ukrainian also as a second language. Because of the-- because of the Ukrainian population in the area, the Polish government was accommodating the Ukrainian population by teaching the general population Ukrainian.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you give me an idea of the size of Drohobycz as a town. Was this a village or a city or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Drohobycz was a district town where Schodnica was located. Drohobycz had a population of approximately 40,000 to 50,000, out of which about 20,000 were Jewish. Drohobycz had the biggest oil refinery in Poland called Polmin. And the-- and that refinery, there was another refinery also called Galicia. And-- and the third refinery was Nafta. But Polmin was the biggest refinery in Poland. Was refining the production of oil to the gasoline from-- you know, from-- and other byproducts, other petroleum byproducts. And was-- it's pretty important industrial center in Poland, was supplying fuel to entire country. It was the only area where oil was produced in Poland. Was enough for the production for-- for supplying oil for the whole country.

speaker: interviewer
text: Maybe I have not understood right. Where was your school located, in what town? Was it in Drohobycz, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: The public school was in Schodnica.

speaker: interviewer
text: Schodnica.

speaker: interviewee
text: The public school was in Schodnica. But when I finished public school, about the age of 13 or 14, I-- I-- I had to go to-- to-- to a so-called gymnasium. It was in in-- lyceum. Lyceum, which was a middle school, high school. And where you graduated to be accepted to a university. Actually, the equivalent of the baccalaureate or the graduation of the high school, of lyceum, in-- in Poland was equivalent to two years of college in America. So we had a little jump start. Those years that I went in Drohobycz to gymnasium were very-- many happy years. I went to Jewish school called-- it was-- the-- the name of the school of Professor Leon Sternberg. But being the-- the director of the school was-- was a-- a Professor Blatt. So colloquially, it was called the Blatt's Gymnasium. That's where I spent the next six years of my life. I-- the first four years are still till the outbreak of the war was gymnasium. And then when the-- when the war broke out and the Russians came, it was still the same school and I continued with two more years, the lyceum, where I graduated. And as a matter of fact, I was even matriculated at-- into medical school in Lwow a-- a week before the-- the Russo-German war broke out in 1941. And being I was a top student, I was accepted without the entrance exam.

speaker: interviewer
text: I would like to-- I would like for you to do something for me. Give me the years, please, of your elementary school years, and then your lyceum gymnasium years so that we can establish what-- what year we were talking about.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Elementary school years were 1929, when I turned six, 1929 till about the year of 1935. In 1935, I started high school gymnasium, till 1939. Four years. And I ended with-- gymnasium was four years. And I ended in-- in 1939. And in 1941-- 19-- I'm sorry. In 1939, in the fall was after-- right after the outbreak of the war, when the Russians came, I started lyceum. The same school. Until 1941. June 1941. At the end of the school year.

speaker: interviewer
text: I would like to go back for a minute, back to your life at home.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Schodnica.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Schodnica. Would you tell me how-- for instance, how a typical Friday night and Shabbos would be spent by you and your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: That, I'll never forget. Those memorable days of-- of my life. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] See, I'm a little emotional. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] When we sat down at the table, the Sabbath table Friday night, [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] my grandfather was sitting at-- at the head of the table. Next to him, my-- my grandmother, then my parents. And me and my sister. There was always had to be a poor person, at least a poor person, participating, invited to share with us the Sabbath meal. Usually, we were-- we were getting [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] a-- a guest in the synagogue. Usually poor people were wandering from one town to another. And after the Friday night services, my grandfather was picking up a couple of wanderers, poor people, to share the Sabbath meal with us. I remember particularly one wintry night [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] where it was a-- a big storm. Because we were in the snow-- the snow belt area, with the mountains, the Carpathian Mountains there. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] there were no people, no strangers in our-- in our synagogue. And I looked at my grandfather's face. He was very sad. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So after the services, I disappeared from the synagogue and started looking in other synagogues for a-- for a poor person. Luckily enough, I found one. Not maybe a half an hour later, 45 minutes later, I came to the house with this poor person in tow.

speaker: interviewee
text: I walked in. My parents, my grandparents were very worried where I was. There's a big snow. That they were afraid that maybe I got stuck in the snow someplace. And here I walk in with this poor person in tow, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and my grandfather looked at me and I saw tears coming down his eyes. [CRYING] He was very happy. And sit down. He kissed me. He thanked me. This is one event I'll never forget as long as I live. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Usually, the-- the Sabbath service is a very happy-- very happy event. We were singing songs. My mother was preparing very nice meals. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My-- my mother and my grandmother were both very good cooks. And-- [PAUSES FOR 14 SECONDS] And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] so these-- these Sabbath days are very-- very important in my memories. I remember Saturday morning, for instance, my father-- there were two services, the early service at like 7 o'clock in the morning. The next one was like 9 o'clock in the morning. My father, being that he was an early riser, he preferred to go to the 7 o'clock services. And I was going with him. So we came home about 9:00, 9:30. We were home already. So we had-- my father made a-- a blessing in wine, and we ate a little-- some appetizer, some chopped liver or something like that. And we went for a walk, if weather permitting, my mother, my sister, my father, myself. And we walked usually to the-- in the direction of the oil fields. And we had also-- on-- on the way, we had our own fields too. So my father was showing off our fields, our wheat, and the potatoes over there growing. It was a very nice walk in the-- through the fields to the-- to the oil fields. And this is how we usually spend the nice mornings of Saturday. Then we came home. And we had a Saturday, the Sabbath meal. And usually my-- my father, being that he was very tired, worked very hard during the week, he always preferred to have a Saturday afternoon nap. It was napping Saturday afternoon. And while I was playing outdoors with my-- with my friends.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mention your fields. Did your father employ people to work in those fields?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes, definitely. He employed because he had no time, so he was not a-- an agricultural expert. And he hired Ukrainian help, either to-- to till the fields and to [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] put the-- seed them after they were tilled. And also to-- to get the crap out of the fields. But I remember when the war broke out in '41, and we were already-- I'll say it later. We were already moved out of Schodnica to the Drohobycz, because Schodnica to be Judenrein. I went-- and we-- we knew we had potatoes left in the field in Schodnica. It was very dangerous to go back there. But somehow, we had enough courage, my father and I. And we went to Schodnica secretly, and we dug out some potatoes in the fields. And with a little hand wagon, we dragged those potatoes with us maybe 30 kilometers to the city, to Drohobycz, to-- to be able to survive. But as it turned out, they were frozen in that winter in '42, and we didn't have even the-- those potatoes either.

speaker: interviewer
text: The stranger that you brought to have the Shabbat bought meal with you-- with you, did he stay with you that whole day? Could you tell me about that, or did he leave, or what happened with this-- with a person like that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Usually when we had a person like this, we made accommodations for him to sleep over the night, and he stayed the following day with us too. The whole Sabbath. He was-- he was ours for the whole Sabbath.

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHS] All right. We-- we're getting to the point where [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] you are going to school in Drohobycz. Could you tell me a little bit about this? Were you a commuter? Did you go back and forth? What was the distance? What--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I could not commute. I had to-- my father arranged-- he rented for me facilities, like a room with a family in Drohobycz. And made sure that it's a family observes-- observes kosher, observes the dietary laws. They didn't want me to-- to stray from our religion. And he-- my father paid for it. And I had room and board. And I was usually within walking distance from the school. And I-- I was coming home then only either for the holidays or for vacations. And I was there. All year, I was staying in Drohobycz and studying.

speaker: interviewer
text: You-- please clarify for me. I don't quite think I understood. The school that you went to, the gymnasium lyceum, was this a Jewish school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. It was a Jewish school. It was 100%-- although--

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you describe--

speaker: interviewee
text: --a couple of-- a couple of Gentiles chose to register in the same school too. But there was obviously the majority was Jewish. I would say 90%, 95%.
